question_title: Will AfD win the most seats in the Baden-Württemberg Landtag election?
summary: The AfD is polling at ~20% in Baden-Württemberg, trailing the CDU at ~29% by approximately 9 percentage points across all recent polls (INSA, Infratest dimap, PolitPro trend). Seat projections show CDU at ~38 seats vs AfD at ~26 seats (12-seat gap out of 120). The Grüne are also polling above AfD at ~22%. With only 17 days until the March 8 election, closing a 9pp gap would be essentially unprecedented in German state elections. Monte Carlo simulations with realistic polling error assumptions (σ=2.5pp) show AfD winning most seats in under 1% of scenarios. Even accounting for possible systematic AfD underestimation of 3pp, the probability only reaches ~2%. The new MMP electoral system introduces mild uncertainty but proportional systems closely track vote shares. Setting the probability at 3% to account for unknown unknowns and tail risks.

factors.description: CDU consistently leads polls at ~29% vs AfD at ~20% - a 9pp gap confirmed by multiple pollsters (INSA, Infratest dimap) with 17 days to election
Seat projections show CDU at ~38 seats vs AfD at ~26 seats (12-seat gap out of 120 total). Gap is robust across projection models.
Election is only 17 days away - limited time for dramatic polling shift. Late surges of 9pp are essentially unprecedented in German state elections.
Grüne at ~22% also polling above AfD. AfD would need to beat ALL parties, not just CDU, making the hurdle even higher.
CDU has structural advantages in BW constituencies as traditional stronghold. Even under new MMP system, likely retains constituency bonus.
Possible systematic AfD underestimate in polls (shy voter effect). Historical evidence suggests 2-3pp possible but insufficient to close 9pp gap.
New two-vote MMP electoral system used for first time in BW. Some uncertainty in vote-to-seat translation, but proportional systems are well-understood.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 3% probability that AfD wins the most seats in the 2026 Baden-Württemberg Landtag election.**

Key reasoning:
- **Massive polling gap**: CDU leads AfD by ~9 percentage points (29% vs 20%) across all recent polls, translating to a projected 12-seat gap (38 vs 26 out of 120)
- **Double hurdle**: AfD also trails the Grüne (~22%), so they must surpass both parties
- **Time constraint**: With only 17 days until the March 8 election, a 9pp swing would be essentially unprecedented in German state elections
- **Monte Carlo validation**: Simulations with realistic polling errors (σ=2.5pp) show AfD winning most seats in <1% of scenarios; even with a generous 3pp systematic AfD underestimate, probability only reaches ~2%
- The 3% accounts for unknown unknowns and extreme tail scenarios (major CDU scandal, systematic polling failure, etc.)
